articles.title: The two oldest printing presses
articles.author: janpot
articles.summary:
The article highlights the significance of the world's two oldest printing presses, located at the Plantin-Moretus Museum, which date back to around 1600. These presses were instrumental in revolutionizing communication by dramatically increasing printing speed. During Christoffel Plantin's era, the printing workshop was a bustling hub with sixteen presses and 56 workers, producing up to 1,250 sheets daily over long working hours, underscoring the labor-intensive nature of historical printing.

Today, these two oldest wooden printing presses, while no longer operational, serve as a testament to the rich history of print technology. Visitors to the museum can experience this legacy, which includes well-preserved printing facilities and showcases other cultural artifacts, illustrating how communication via print has evolved long before the digital age. The Plantin-Moretus Museum not only preserves these historical presses but also encapsulates a critical part of the evolution of the dissemination of knowledge.

articles.title: Type out the code
articles.author: Gabriella439
articles.summary:
The article emphasizes the importance of actively typing out code versus merely copying and pasting it, asserting that this practice significantly enhances comprehension and programming skills. By advocating for the deletion of completed exercises and their reconstruction from memory, the author aligns with the cognitive psychology principle known as the generation effect, which illustrates that generating content fosters better retention and understanding than passively consuming it. This approach not only aids memory recall but also cultivates fluency in the various aspects of programming, such as syntax, structure, and key terminology.

Moreover, the article highlights the significance of 'freecoding,' where programmers practice coding without reliance on tools, thereby solidifying foundational knowledge. The author warns against an over-reliance on coding agents in modern programming, stressing that maintaining traditional coding capabilities is essential for future generations of programmers. Overall, the piece serves as a call to action for programmers to prioritize active engagement with coding techniques to ensure their skills remain sharp in an evolving technological landscape.

articles.title: Comprehensive Response to Bambu's AGPLv3 Violations
articles.author: susanthenerd
articles.summary:
The Software Freedom Conservancy (SFC) has announced a comprehensive initiative to address copyright violations committed by Bambu Lab regarding their 3D printer software under the Affero General Public License version 3 (AGPLv3). Following a compliance investigation, SFC identified confirmed violations, particularly related to Bambu Lab's failure to provide the complete Corresponding Source Code for their Slicer software, known as Bambu Studio. This software is derived from a modified version of the AGPLv3 licensed PrusaSlicer, which has raised significant concerns regarding user rights to modify and access software properly.

As part of their response, SFC is actively seeking donations to support efforts aimed at enforcing software right-to-repair and ensuring compliance with AGPLv3. Donations collected until July 17 will directly fund this work, and thus far, SFC has raised $23,786 towards a goal of $226,221. While the compliance investigation continues, SFC emphasizes the importance of protecting user rights in the 3D printing community, advocating for immediate and long-term solutions to the violating practices of Bambu Lab.

articles.title: Haiku OS runs on M1 Macs now
articles.author: edwardloveall
articles.summary:
The article discusses the recent compatibility of Haiku OS with Apple M1 Macs, marking a significant milestone for the operating system. The porting process was facilitated by ongoing efforts from the Haiku development team, which is dedicated to enhancing the functionality and reach of the OS. This compatibility not only broadens the user base for Haiku OS but also allows users to leverage the robust processing capabilities of M1 Macs, making it a viable lightweight operating system alternative.

As the Haiku OS community continues to evolve, this development highlights the increasing interest in alternative operating systems, particularly on modern hardware. The ability to run Haiku OS on M1 systems indicates a dedicated effort towards improving hardware compatibility and reflects the ongoing commitment to providing users with a simple and efficient computing experience. Overall, this initiative underscores the potential for Haiku OS to make a significant impact in the landscape of operating systems.

articles.title: OpenBSD 7.9 released
articles.author: utzig
articles.summary:
The release of OpenBSD 7.9 on May 19, 2026, showcases the ongoing development and improvements of the OpenBSD operating system, celebrating its 60th version. This release includes platform-specific enhancements across various architectures, such as arm64, amd64, luna88k, and riscv64, emphasizing support for newer hardware components and advanced features. Key improvements involve adding support for new System on Chips (SoCs), maximizing CPU configurations, and addressing specific bugs that could affect performance and stability.

OpenBSD 7.9 also brings critical kernel advancements and a collection of fixes that solidify the OS's reputation for security and reliability. The adjustment in CPU core management allows for better performance distribution among cores, while the overall attention to platform-specific needs reflects the developers' commitment to maintaining OpenBSD's robust functionality. Users are encouraged to refer to the changelog for a thorough examination of specific changes from the previous release.

articles.title: Better generated branch names with jj
articles.author: pushcx
articles.summary:
The article discusses the jj version control system, a Git-compatible tool that automatically generates branch names which may be unclear and cumbersome. The author highlights the necessity for more meaningful branch names, especially when examining branches in a collaborative environment like GitHub. By implementing a custom template alias called slugify(), the author demonstrates how to produce more readable and relevant branch names that seamlessly incorporate change descriptions alongside revision IDs. 

This enhancement aids in enhancing clarity when tracking changes, particularly useful in identifying the purpose of branches. The author also acknowledges the need for personal namespace management in shared repositories, while noting the challenges posed by Git’s complicated branch naming conventions, which remain unaddressed in his approach. Overall, the article emphasizes the importance of readable branch names and provides a practical solution for improving usability within the jj system.

articles.title: How we used Quint to find over 10 bugs in SQLite while hardening Turso
articles.author: typesanitizer
articles.summary:
The article explores how Turso, a rewrite of SQLite focused on reliability, leveraged the formal verification tool Quint to identify more than 10 bugs in SQLite while hardening their database solution. By encouraging community participation in testing, particularly through Pavan Nambi's efforts, Turso emphasizes its commitment to maintain high standards of quality. The piece highlights the various testing frameworks employed, including Deterministic Simulation Testing and fuzzers, while acknowledging the challenges faced with formal methods in software development.

Nambi's innovative approach involved modeling the well-specified SQLite C API using Quint, which allowed for effective testing and validation of the system. This initiative not only strengthened Turso's integrity but also showcased the power of formal verification methods in uncovering potential issues in established systems like SQLite. The collaborative aspect of the Turso community plays an essential role in enhancing the database's reliability, setting a promising trajectory for future developments.

articles.title: Introducing Casuarina Linux: A glibc-Based Chimera Linux Derivative
articles.author: wezm
articles.summary:
Casuarina Linux is a new experimental Linux distribution created as a derivative of Chimera Linux, incorporating glibc for broader compatibility with existing GNU/Linux software ecosystems. It seeks to deliver a user-friendly experience while maintaining many features that appeal to desktop users. With its combination of the LLVM toolchain, Dinit init system, and GNU libc, Casuarina Linux promises a compact yet fully functional operating system suitable for various applications.

Currently, the distribution supports the x86_64 architecture, with hopes to expand to aarch64 in the future. Its development has been influenced by the shortcomings of existing musl-based distributions, prompting the creation of Casuarina. The project, which is still in development, has already been successfully used as a daily driver by its creator. Users are welcome to try it and participate in discussions through community forums, reflecting a collaborative approach to its ongoing development.

articles.title: My domain got abused on Github Pages
articles.author: lalitm
articles.summary:
The article discusses the author's alarming experience with domain abuse related to GitHub Pages. While traveling in Africa, the author received a notification from Google Search Console about an unauthorized owner for their domain, leading them to discover that someone had set up a subdomain for malicious purposes. Through exploration, they learned that GitHub’s structure permits anyone to create subdomains as long as a repository exists with a corresponding CNAME file, challenging their previous assumptions about domain ownership and security.

The author highlights the ease with which domains can be abused due to these settings, revealing tools available on GitHub that facilitate such activities. They express concern for potential victims of the scam sites that could have utilized their domain, particularly given that they had been preoccupied with personal projects, which resulted in missed alerts from Google. This article raises significant red flags regarding domain security and the need for better awareness and protective measures when using platforms like GitHub for hosting web content.

articles.title: Spork: A posix_spawn you can use as a fork
articles.author: orib
articles.summary:
The article introduces Spork, a novel approach that integrates the benefits of posix_spawn with the flexibility of fork. This new method aims to simplify process creation while enhancing performance and resource management in multitasking environments. By addressing the inefficiencies traditionally associated with forking, Spork allows developers to create processes more effectively and with fewer errors.

Spork not only improves upon existing methods but also maintains compatibility with current systems, making it an appealing option for developers. The article includes several use cases that highlight Spork's advantages, particularly in high-performance computing scenarios. Overall, Spork represents a significant advancement in process management, promising enhanced performance and easier resource management in various computing applications.

articles.title: Even More Tagged Union Subsets with Comptime
articles.author: sinclairtarget
articles.summary:
The article discusses the utility of Zig's comptime feature in creating tagged union subsets, specifically focusing on examples provided by Mitchell Hashimoto. He demonstrates how this mechanism enables developers to derive more specific unions from generic ones, enhancing the specificity of function parameters and minimizing errors during compile time. The functions defined within these subsets can specifically handle application-wide or terminal-specific keyboard actions, facilitating clearer and more maintainable code.

Additionally, the author shares a personal experience with tagged unions while developing a parser for MyST, a new Markdown format. In this context, a large tagged union represents various node types in the Abstract Syntax Tree (AST), some of which can have children and some which cannot. This leads to different parsing requirements and demonstrates the flexibility and power of using tagged union subsets in complex programming scenarios, ultimately contributing to a more robust and organized code structure.

articles.title: The just-say-no engineer was a ZIRP phenomenon
articles.author: cjoly
articles.summary:
The article discusses the archetype of the 'just-say-no' engineer, who blocks unnecessary code changes to ensure quality and minimize complexity. This contrasting figure to the 'just-say-yes' engineer gained prominence during the zero interest rate policy (ZIRP) era, which allowed tech companies to hire aggressively and prioritize rapid growth without the burden of managing numerous code changes effectively. As AI technologies emerge, these engineers face unprecedented pressure from both AI-generated code and organizational politics, forcing them to reevaluate their strict standards. 

With the end of ZIRP, companies are now challenged to rein in chaotic engineering processes that thrived during the borrowing boom. The traditional roles within software engineering are shifting, highlighting the increasing influence of AI on decision-making in code development and management. The article underscores the importance of quality control in software engineering, particularly as it navigates the delicate balance between innovation and stability.

articles.title: The Super Tiny Compiler, but in Ada
articles.author: tomekw
articles.summary:
The Super Tiny Compiler (STCC) is an innovative project that provides an Ada implementation of a simple compiler, designed to parse and execute basic arithmetic expressions. Developed by Tomek Wałkuski, the compiler showcases the ability to perform operations like addition and subtraction through straightforward input commands that are executed directly in the terminal. This endeavor not only illustrates the pivotal role of compilers in programming but also serves as a valuable resource for understanding the underlying principles of compiler construction within the Ada programming environment.

The project structure includes essential files such as source code, tests, and configuration files, allowing for organized navigation and easy handling by contributors and users. Although it has been developed manually, it has garnered attention from the community, reflected in the 17 stars on its GitHub repository. By providing clear examples and accessibility to compiler functionalities, STCC aims to enhance the learning and practical application of compiler design while promoting the use of Ada as a programming language.

articles.title: The Unreasonable Effectiveness of ProseMirror Model in Rich Text Transformation
articles.author: smoores
articles.summary:
The article discusses the exceptional utility of ProseMirror's data model in rich text transformation, particularly in the context of the Storyteller platform, which aligns ebooks with their corresponding audiobooks. The author, who regularly works with ProseMirror, sheds light on the challenges of synchronizing text and audio, particularly due to the structural limitations of EPUB files, which utilize XHTML and SMIL. This limitation stems from the reliance on URI fragments that only allow synchronization at the element level, which is often insufficient for the needs of detailed alignment.

To effectively synchronize audio beyond the textblock level, the author highlights the necessity of modifying the markup to ensure unique element IDs for each relevant text span. This point underscores ProseMirror's versatility, demonstrating its effectiveness even in applications not directly related to conventional rich text editing. Overall, the article presents a compelling case for the model's adaptability and its potential for improving rich text transformation processes.

articles.title: Under the Hood: Building a Real-Time Chord Recognizer
articles.author: soulcutter
articles.summary:
The article 'Under the Hood: Building a Real-Time Chord Recognizer' details the technical framework behind the WhatChord chord recognition engine, emphasizing the importance of a scoring model over a simple dictionary for identifying chord types. With only 12 pitch classes, there are numerous combinations, but their interpretations can differ based on musical context, necessitating a ranking system to evaluate chord fitting.

The recognition algorithm operates through a four-stage pipeline that processes a set of pitch classes and the lowest note to derive potential chord candidates. It utilizes a 12-bit integer to represent pitch classes, normalizes scores for comparative analysis, and applies musical heuristics for ranking outcomes. The article concludes with insights into the limitations of the system, highlighting the complexity of accurately identifying chords depending on various musical factors.

articles.title: The Windows DLL loader lock: how a Rust thread can hang your JVM
articles.author: jmillikin
articles.summary:
The article addresses a complex issue encountered by developers using a combination of Rust and Java on Windows systems, specifically focusing on a deadlock that can cause sporadic hangs in their Continuous Integration (CI) pipeline. The investigation revealed that the deadlock arises when a Rust thread is trying to detach from the Java Virtual Machine (JVM) while the garbage collector is actively running. The Windows Loader Lock mechanism plays a critical role in this issue, as it prevents new Java threads from initializing when the Rust thread is holding the lock, leading to a standstill where both the Rust thread waits for the garbage collector and vice versa.

Through detailed debugging processes, including examining process dumps and stack traces, the developers laid bare the intricate relationship between Rust's thread-local storage and the JNI attachment protocol. The findings emphasize the importance of managing concurrency and threads effectively, particularly in multi-language applications, to prevent deadlocks and ensure the smooth operation of systems. This insight underscores the challenges posed by cross-language integrations, especially when leveraging powerful features from both Rust and Java in a performance-sensitive environment.

articles.title: Software's Centaur Era
articles.author: carlana
articles.summary:
In the article 'Software's Centaur Era,' Richard Marmorstein explores the implications of AI advancements on knowledge work and its impact on software engineering. By 2030, with AI dominating various tasks, there’s emerging anxiety among students considering computer science, as many fear job automation. However, Marmorstein argues that while software roles will evolve, they will not vanish entirely, emphasizing the importance of human oversight in a landscape increasingly influenced by technology.

The centaur analogy from chess illustrates the transitional phase where human and AI collaboration was effective. Marmorstein highlights that today's AI coding tools are not yet capable of independently navigating long-term projects without risking significant errors. This suggests that, despite the rise of AI, there remains a critical demand for skilled professionals to guide and enhance software development, thereby redefining roles in the industry rather than eliminating them.

articles.title: pgBackRest will continue
articles.author: binarycleric
articles.summary:
pgBackRest, a critical tool for PostgreSQL users, has secured its future with the backing of multiple sponsors, alleviating previous concerns about its maintenance. This coalition, which includes notable companies like Amazon Web Services and Supabase, ensures that the project now enjoys greater stability and sustainability than before, moving away from dependence on a single sponsor. Their support underscores the importance of pgBackRest in the overall PostgreSQL ecosystem, particularly for reliable disaster recovery solutions.

In addition to ongoing development, the pgBackRest community has expressed overwhelming support during a transitional phase when its future was uncertain. Positive feedback from users indicates a strong desire for continued enhancements and optimizations that are planned for future releases. With a solid financial foundation and an active community backing the project, pgBackRest is poised for growth and innovation, further solidifying its essential role for developers relying on PostgreSQL.

articles.title: Pokemon Gen2 compression myth
articles.author: juliethefoxcoon
articles.summary:
The article addresses the persistent myth surrounding the compression techniques used in Pokémon Gen 2, highlighting the misconceptions associated with Satoru Iwata's contributions. It emphasizes that while Iwata made significant strides in the compression of game data, the reality of what was achieved is often exaggerated or misunderstood. Many fans and followers of the series hold onto the belief that every piece of data underwent complex compression processes, which overlooks the narrative aspect of development decisions.

Furthermore, the article encourages readers to delve deeper into the history and technicalities of game development, promoting a more nuanced understanding of how popular games are crafted. By challenging the myths and discussing the true nature of these development techniques, it advocates for a more informed appreciation of the artistry and technical skills involved in video game creation, ultimately enriching the gaming community's knowledge and experience.

articles.title: I am not a Software Engineer
articles.author: l0b0
articles.summary:
The article expresses the author's long-standing position as someone who identifies more as a hacker than a software engineer, a distinction they find significant. Over 23 years, the author's perspective has been shaped by a belief in the importance of readable, understandable code and skepticism towards contemporary trends in software engineering that prioritize machine-generated output over human comprehension. This has led to feelings of disconnection from the prevailing industry standards, described as the 'new agentic paradigm'.

Throughout the piece, the author conveys a sense of bewilderment regarding the current focus on automated agents in software development, particularly the preference for natural language user interfaces and the shift away from traditional coding practices. While the author acknowledges the perceived expertise of current software engineers, they argue for the value of foundational knowledge, critical dialogue, and a commitment to reproducibility in coding. Ultimately, the article serves as a reflection on the evolution of the field and the author's ongoing struggle to reconcile their beliefs with modern practices.

articles.title: Human Bottlenecks
articles.author: puhsu
articles.summary:
The article 'Human Bottlenecks' explores the unmet potential of AI models in augmenting human capabilities, emphasizing that while these tools are powerful, there exists a significant gap between their capabilities and actual utilization by individuals. The author points out that many users feel they are underusing AI, envisioning it as a means to enhance productivity and learning, yet the reality shows that many have not established a solid context for effectively integrating AI into their daily practices. The desires for AI-driven solutions often collide with a lack of authentic engagement in the foundational activities that these technologies aim to assist with, resulting in a disconnect. 

Further, the article discusses the internal barriers individuals face, such as the absence of a personal need to learn or improve, which leads to a rational avoidance of exploring AI applications. This is compounded by the challenge of opportunity costs related to learning and the lack of immediate incentives to dive into self-directed study. Ultimately, the piece reflects on the historical ambitions for computational tools to enrich human intellect while acknowledging that bottlenecks in motivation and serious usage contexts continue to hinder meaningful engagement with these powerful technologies.

articles.title: Chasing down why installing the kernel segfaulted
articles.author: calvin
articles.summary:
The article examines a segmentation fault encountered during a kernel installation on a server aimed at continuous integration for a specific architecture. These issues often arise from frequent kernel patches due to well-known vulnerabilities like Copy Fail, accentuating the need for diligent management of server infrastructure. In this case, the fault manifests during the execution of a dracut command within a Gentoo-specific installation script while handling hardlinking of files, prompting the author to delve into the associated scripts to identify the underlying problem.

By enabling verbose output in the installation process, the author attempts to gather more insights into the error's origin. The investigation reveals a potential link to the dracut tool, particularly as it pertains to generating the initramfs. To resolve the issue, the author suggests using the GDB debugger for a deeper analysis, underscoring the challenges faced in managing kernel installations and the importance of utilizing effective debugging strategies in troubleshooting such critical system components.

articles.title: Tonic is joining the gRPC project
articles.author: ajdecon
articles.summary:
The article details the transition of Tonic, a Rust-based gRPC library, as it becomes part of the official gRPC project under the CNCF. This change is significant for the Rust programming community, providing enhanced support and resources for developers using gRPC in their applications. The history of Tonic is outlined, highlighting its inception in 2019, the challenges regarding maintenance and feature development as the project grew, and the dependencies that complicated its upkeep.

As the project faced increasing maintenance burdens and a lack of dedicated contributors, a collaboration with the Google gRPC team emerged in 2024 aimed at developing gRPC-Rust. This strategic partnership is expected to address existing challenges and facilitate further innovation in the Rust gRPC ecosystem, ensuring better development support and sustainability for users.
